skill.name: astropy
skill.description: Guides astronomy and astrophysics work with Astropy, including units, coordinates, FITS files, cosmology, time systems, tables, WCS, and related analysis workflows.
skill.summary: Use Astropy for precise astronomy data analysis in Python.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many severe patterns, but review found they are markdown Python examples and astronomy terminology, not executable skill code. File access and network examples are legitimate Astropy workflows for FITS files, catalog data, and documentation links, so the residual risk is low and user-data dependent.

low_findings.title: Markdown Examples Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings point to fenced Python and bash examples in documentation. They are not executable hooks in the skill package, and no command injection path was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are markdown fenced examples or ordinary imports. I found no executable wrapper, script file, shell interpolation, or user-controlled command path.

low_findings.title: Legitimate FITS File Operations Require User Data Care
low_findings.description: The skill documents opening, updating, writing, and memory-mapping FITS files. This is expected for Astropy, but users should apply it only to intended astronomy files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are real capabilities, but they are normal Astropy examples. No sensitive file targeting or unauthorized filesystem traversal was found.

low_findings.title: Network References Are Documentation and Data Download Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are official Astropy resources and an example data URL. The download example is transparent and cache-oriented, with no credential collection or hidden exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network use is explicitly documented and limited to public documentation or example data retrieval. I found no secret access or covert destination.

low_findings.title: SAMP Astronomy Protocol Misclassified as Windows SAM Access
low_findings.description: The critical Windows SAM findings are false positives caused by Astropy SAMP documentation. The text describes Simple Application Messaging Protocol for astronomy tool interoperability.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The heading expands SAMP as Simple Application Messaging Protocol and the code uses astropy.samp. There is no Windows SAM database path or credential extraction behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak-Crypto and C2 Keyword Alerts Are Domain-Term Collisions
low_findings.description: The weak-crypto, command-and-control, and reconnaissance alerts map to astronomy abbreviations, FITS header keywords, magnitude notation, or performance notes. No malicious security behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed contexts use astronomy vocabulary such as distance modulus, decibels, header cards, and Earth rotation angle. I found no cryptography, beaconing, or host reconnaissance intent.

content.value_statement: Astronomy work often needs precise units, coordinates, time systems, and FITS handling. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Astropy workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains Astropy units, quantities, equivalencies, and unit-aware calculations.
Guides celestial coordinate creation, transformations, separation checks, and catalog matching.
Shows FITS file reading, header inspection, data access, writing, and large-file handling.
Covers cosmology models, distances, ages, lookback times, and redshift calculations.
Supports Astropy time scales, table operations, WCS transformations, and visualization helpers.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not a bundled executable analysis pipeline.
It assumes users have Python and Astropy installed in their working environment.
It cannot validate the scientific quality of input data without domain context.
Remote data examples depend on network access and the availability of external sources.

use_cases.title: Reduce Telescope Data
use_cases.description: Open FITS images, inspect headers, read data arrays, and prepare astronomy files for analysis.

use_cases.title: Match Sky Catalogs
use_cases.description: Transform coordinates, compare separations, and cross-match tables with unit-aware thresholds.

use_cases.title: Teach Astropy Workflows
use_cases.description: Create clear examples for units, time systems, cosmology, WCS, and astronomical tables.

prompt_templates.title: Convert Basic Units
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Astropy to convert these astronomy values between units: [values]. Explain each conversion and note any required equivalencies.

prompt_templates.title: Transform Coordinates
prompt_templates.prompt: Build an Astropy workflow that transforms these coordinates from [source frame] to [target frame] and reports angular separations.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect FITS Data
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through reading [file name] with Astropy, inspecting HDUs and headers, and summarizing the image or table data.

prompt_templates.title: Design an Astronomy Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an Astropy analysis plan that combines FITS I/O, WCS conversion, catalog matching, time handling, and cosmology calculations for [research goal].

output_examples.input: Convert a source position from ICRS to Galactic coordinates and compare it with a catalog position.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step coordinate workflow with required imports, frame choices, unit handling, and a plain-language interpretation of the separation result.

output_examples.input: Inspect a FITS image and summarize exposure time, filter, dimensions, and basic statistics.
output_examples.output: A concise analysis outline that opens the FITS file safely, identifies the relevant HDU, reads header metadata, and reports image statistics.

output_examples.input: Calculate luminosity distance and lookback time for several redshifts using a Planck cosmology.
output_examples.output: A reproducible cosmology workflow that states the selected model, expected units, and how to present distances and times in a table.

best_practices: Attach Astropy units to physical values before calculations or comparisons.
Use context managers when opening FITS files to close resources reliably.
State coordinate frames, time scales, and cosmology models explicitly.
anti_patterns: Do not mix raw numbers with unit-aware quantities without checking dimensions.
Do not assume FITS extension order without inspecting the HDU list first.
Do not transform observer-dependent coordinates without time and location data.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps users apply Astropy to astronomy data, coordinates, FITS files, cosmology, time systems, tables, and WCS workflows.

faq.question: Does it install Astropy automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides installation guidance, but users must choose when to install packages in their environment.

faq.question: Can it analyze FITS files?
faq.answer: It can guide FITS reading, header inspection, data extraction, writing, and memory mapping through Astropy examples.

faq.question: Does it support coordinate transformations?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers SkyCoord, common frames, observer-dependent frames, separations, and catalog matching patterns.

faq.question: Can it work with remote astronomy data?
faq.answer: It includes examples for documented Astropy remote data access, but users should approve network use and verify sources.

faq.question: Is it safe for sensitive data?
faq.answer: The skill has no hidden execution code, but users should avoid sharing private files or credentials in analysis prompts.
